---
title: "Function reference"
---

All functions and classes exported by ti.

## Functions

| Function | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| [`abc()`](#abc) | ABC classification function |
| [`abc_fn()`](#abc_fn) | Classify a group by proportion of a variable (A,B,C,...) |
| [`atd()`](#atd) | All period-to-date |
| [`atd_fn()`](#atd_fn) | All-to-date execution function |
| [`augment_standard_calendar()`](#augment_standard_calendar) | Add Comprehensive Date-Based Attributes to a DBI  lazy frame or tibble object |
| [`augment_standard_calendar_dbi()`](#augment_standard_calendar_dbi) | Add Comprehensive Date-Based Attributes to a DBI lazy frame |
| [`augment_standard_calendar_tbl()`](#augment_standard_calendar_tbl) | Add Comprehensive Date-Based Attributes to a Data Frame |
| [`calculate()`](#calculate) | Execute time-intelligence or segments class objects to return the underlying tra |
| [`cohort()`](#cohort) | Cohort Analysis |
| [`cohort_fn()`](#cohort_fn) | Internal function for the cohort segment object |
| [`complete_standard_calendar()`](#complete_standard_calendar) | Title |
| [`create_calendar()`](#create_calendar) | Create Calendar Table |
| [`create_full_dbi()`](#create_full_dbi) | Title |
| [`detect_sql_dialect()`](#detect_sql_dialect) | Detect SQL dialect from a DBI connection |
| [`dod()`](#dod) | Current period day over previous period day |
| [`dod_fn()`](#dod_fn) | Day-over-day execution function |
| [`generate_cli_action()`](#generate_cli_action) | Generate CLI actions |
| [`generate_retail_calendar()`](#generate_retail_calendar) | Generate a retail (4-4-5, 4-5-4, or 5-4-4) calendar mapping table |
| [`is_yyyy_mm_dd()`](#is_yyyy_mm_dd) | Validate an input is YYYY-MM-DD format |
| [`make_action_cli()`](#make_action_cli) | Make Action field CLI args |
| [`make_db_tbl()`](#make_db_tbl) | Make an in memory database from a table |
| [`mom()`](#mom) | Current full period month over previous full period month |
| [`mom_fn()`](#mom_fn) | month-over-month execution function |
| [`momtd()`](#momtd) | Current period month to date compared to previous period month-to-date |
| [`momtd_fn()`](#momtd_fn) | Current year to date over previous year-to-date for tibble objects |
| [`mtd()`](#mtd) | Current period month-to-date |
| [`mtd_fn()`](#mtd_fn) | Month-to-date execution function |
| [`mtdopm()`](#mtdopm) | Current month-to-date over full previous period month |
| [`mtdopm_fn()`](#mtdopm_fn) | Month-to-date over full previous month execution function |
| [`pmtd()`](#pmtd) | Previous period month-to-date |
| [`pmtd_fn()`](#pmtd_fn) | Previous month-to-date execution function |
| [`pqtd()`](#pqtd) | Prior period quarter-to-date |
| [`pqtd_fn()`](#pqtd_fn) | Previous quarter-to-date for tibble objects |
| [`print()`](#print) | Print ti objects |
| [`print_actions_steps()`](#print_actions_steps) | Print action steps |
| [`print_calendar_info()`](#print_calendar_info) | Print calendar information block |
| [`print_fn_info()`](#print_fn_info) | Prints function header info |
| [`print_next_steps()`](#print_next_steps) | Prints functions next steps |
| [`pwtd()`](#pwtd) | Previous period week-to-date |
| [`pwtd_fn()`](#pwtd_fn) | Previous week-to-date execution function |
| [`pytd()`](#pytd) | Previous period year-to-date |
| [`pytd_fn()`](#pytd_fn) | Previous year-to-date execution function |
| [`qoq()`](#qoq) | Current full period quarter over previous full period quarter |
| [`qoq_fn()`](#qoq_fn) | Quarter-over-quarter execution function |
| [`qoqtd()`](#qoqtd) | Current period quarter-to-date compared to previous period quarter-to-date |
| [`qoqtd_fn()`](#qoqtd_fn) | Current quarter to date over previous quarter-to-date for tibble objects |
| [`qtd()`](#qtd) | Current period quarter-to-date |
| [`qtd_fn()`](#qtd_fn) | Quarter-to-date execution function |
| [`qtdopq()`](#qtdopq) | Current period quarter-to-date over previous period quarter |
| [`qtdopq_fn()`](#qtdopq_fn) | quarter-to-date over previous quarter execution function |
| [`seq_date_sql()`](#seq_date_sql) | Generate a Cross-Dialect SQL Date Series |
| [`sql_date_add()`](#sql_date_add) | Generate dialect-appropriate date addition SQL |
| [`wow()`](#wow) | Current full period week over full previous period week |
| [`wow_fn()`](#wow_fn) | Week-over-week execution function |
| [`wowtd()`](#wowtd) | Current period Week-to-date over previous period week-to-date |
| [`wowtd_fn()`](#wowtd_fn) | Week-to-date over previous week-to-date execution function |
| [`wtd()`](#wtd) | Current period week-to-date |
| [`wtd_fn()`](#wtd_fn) | Week-to-date execution function |
| [`wtdopw()`](#wtdopw) | Current period week-to-date over full previous period week |
| [`wtdopw_fn()`](#wtdopw_fn) | Week-to-date over full previous week execution function |
| [`yoy()`](#yoy) | Current full period year over previous full period year |
| [`yoy_fn()`](#yoy_fn) | Current year-to-date over previous period year-to-date execution function |
| [`yoytd()`](#yoytd) | Current period year-to-date compared to previous period year-to-date |
| [`yoytd_fn()`](#yoytd_fn) | Current year to date over previous year-to-date execution function |
| [`ytd()`](#ytd) | Current period year-to-date |
| [`ytd_fn()`](#ytd_fn) | Year-to-date execution function |
| [`ytdopy()`](#ytdopy) | Current period year-to-date compared to full previous period |
| [`ytdopy_fn()`](#ytdopy_fn) | Year-to-date over full prior period year |

***

## Function Details

### `abc()` {#abc}

ABC classification function

#### Usage

```r
abc(.data, category_values, .value)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `category_values` | vector of break points between 0 and 1 |
| `.value` | optional: if left blank,abc() will use the number of rows per group to categorize, alternatively you can pass a column name to categorize |

#### Value

abc object

#### Description

For your group variable, abc()  will categorize which groups make up what proportion of the totals according to the category_values that you have entered
 The function returns a segment object which prints out the execution steps and actions that it will take to categorize your data
 Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function is helpful to understand which groups of make up what proportion of the cumulative contribution
 If you do not provide a .value then it will count the transactions per group, if you provide .value then it will sum() the .value per group
 The function creates a segment object, which pre-processes the data into its components

#### Examples

```r
contoso::sales |>
  dplyr::group_by(product_key) |>
  abc(c(.1,.5,.7,.96,1), .value = margin)
```

***

### `abc_fn()` {#abc_fn}

Classify a group by proportion of a variable (A,B,C,...)

#### Usage

```r
abc_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | segment object |

#### Value

a dbi objection

#### Description

This returns a table that will segment your data into A,B or C segments based on custom
thresholds

***

### `atd()` {#atd}

All period-to-date

#### Usage

```r
atd(.data, .date, .value, calendar_type = "standard", fiscal_year_start = 1)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the all-time cumulative sum of targeted value using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()

Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
atd(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard")
```

***

### `atd_fn()` {#atd_fn}

All-to-date execution function

#### Usage

```r
atd_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

atd_fn() is the function that is called by atd() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `augment_standard_calendar()` {#augment_standard_calendar}

Add Comprehensive Date-Based Attributes to a DBI  lazy frame or tibble object

#### Usage

```r
augment_standard_calendar(.data, .date)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | A data frame or tibble containing at least one date column. |
| `.date` | A column containing Date values, passed using tidy-eval (e.g., {{ date_col ). |

#### Value

A dbi or  tibble containing the original data along with all generated
date-based attributes.

#### Description

This function takes a data frame and a date column and generates a wide set of
derived date attributes. These include start/end dates for year, quarter,
month, and week; day-of-week indicators; completed and remaining days in each
time unit; and additional convenience variables such as weekend indicators.

It is designed for time-based feature engineering and is useful in reporting,
forecasting, time-series modeling, and data enrichment workflows.

#### Details

The function creates the following groups of attributes:

1. Start and end dates

 year_start_date, year_end_date
 quarter_start_date, quarter_end_date
 month_start_date, month_end_date
 week_start_date, week_end_date

2. Day-of-week fields

 day_of_week – numeric day of the week (1–7)
 day_of_week_label – ordered factor label (e.g., Mon, Tue, …)

3. Duration fields

 days_in_year – total days in the year interval
 days_in_quarter – total days in the quarter interval
 days_in_month – total days in the month

4. Completed/remaining days

 days_complete_in_week, days_remaining_in_week
 days_complete_in_month, days_remaining_in_month
 days_complete_in_quarter, days_remaining_in_quarter
 days_complete_in_year, days_remaining_in_year

5. Miscellaneous

 weekend_indicator – equals 1 if Saturday or Sunday; otherwise 0

All date-derived fields ending in _date are coerced to class Date.

***

### `augment_standard_calendar_dbi()` {#augment_standard_calendar_dbi}

Add Comprehensive Date-Based Attributes to a DBI lazy frame

#### Usage

```r
augment_standard_calendar_dbi(.data, .date)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | A data frame or tibble containing at least one date column. |
| `.date` | A column containing Date values, passed using tidy-eval (e.g., {{ date_col ). |

#### Value

A dbi containing the original data along with all generated
date-based attributes.

#### Description

This function takes a data frame and a date column and generates a wide set of
derived date attributes. These include start/end dates for year, quarter,
month, and week; day-of-week indicators; completed and remaining days in each
time unit; and additional convenience variables such as weekend indicators.

It is designed for time-based feature engineering and is useful in reporting,
forecasting, time-series modeling, and data enrichment workflows.

#### Details

The function creates the following groups of attributes:

1. Start and end dates

 year_start_date, year_end_date
 quarter_start_date, quarter_end_date
 month_start_date, month_end_date
 week_start_date, week_end_date

2. Day-of-week fields

 day_of_week – numeric day of the week (1–7)
 day_of_week_label – ordered factor label (e.g., Mon, Tue, …)

3. Duration fields

 days_in_year – total days in the year interval
 days_in_quarter – total days in the quarter interval
 days_in_month – total days in the month

4. Completed/remaining days

 days_complete_in_week, days_remaining_in_week
 days_complete_in_month, days_remaining_in_month
 days_complete_in_quarter, days_remaining_in_quarter
 days_complete_in_year, days_remaining_in_year

5. Miscellaneous

 weekend_indicator – equals 1 if Saturday or Sunday; otherwise 0

All date-derived fields ending in _date are coerced to class Date.

***

### `augment_standard_calendar_tbl()` {#augment_standard_calendar_tbl}

Add Comprehensive Date-Based Attributes to a Data Frame

#### Usage

```r
augment_standard_calendar_tbl(.data, .date)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | A data frame or tibble containing at least one date column. |
| `.date` | A column containing Date values, passed using tidy-eval (e.g., {{ date_col ). |

#### Value

A tibble containing the original data along with all generated
date-based attributes.

#### Description

This function takes a data frame and a date column and generates a wide set of
derived date attributes. These include start/end dates for year, quarter,
month, and week; day-of-week indicators; completed and remaining days in each
time unit; and additional convenience variables such as weekend indicators.

It is designed for time-based feature engineering and is useful in reporting,
forecasting, time-series modeling, and data enrichment workflows.

#### Details

The function creates the following groups of attributes:

1. Start and end dates

 year_start_date, year_end_date
 quarter_start_date, quarter_end_date
 month_start_date, month_end_date
 week_start_date, week_end_date

2. Day-of-week fields

 day_of_week – numeric day of the week (1–7)
 day_of_week_label – ordered factor label (e.g., Mon, Tue, …)

3. Duration fields

 days_in_year – total days in the year interval
 days_in_quarter – total days in the quarter interval
 days_in_month – total days in the month

4. Completed/remaining days

 days_complete_in_week, days_remaining_in_week
 days_complete_in_month, days_remaining_in_month
 days_complete_in_quarter, days_remaining_in_quarter
 days_complete_in_year, days_remaining_in_year

5. Miscellaneous

 weekend_indicator – equals 1 if Saturday or Sunday; otherwise 0

All date-derived fields ending in _date are coerced to class Date.

***

### `calculate()` {#calculate}

Execute time-intelligence or segments class objects to return the underlying transformed table

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

The calculate() function takes an object created by a time function (like ytd(), mtd(), or qtd()) or a segment function (like cohort() or abc()) and executes the underlying transformation logic.
It translates the function blueprint into an actionable query, returning the final data table.

#### Details

The TI and segment functions in ti—such as ytd() or cohort() and others—are designed to be lazy and database-friendly.
They do not perform the heavy data transformation immediately.
Instead, they return a blueprint object (of class ti,segment_abc or segment_cohort) that contains all the parameters and logic needed for the calculation.

calculate() serves as the execution engine.

When called, it interprets the blueprint and generates optimized R code or SQL code (using the dbplyr package) that is then executed efficiently on the data source, whether it's an in-memory tibble or a remote database backend (like duckdb or snowflake).
This approach minimizes data transfer and improves performance for large datasets.

The resulting table will be sorted by the relevant date column to ensure the correct temporal ordering of the calculated metrics.

#### Examples

```r
x <- ytd(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard")
calculate(x)
```

***

### `cohort()` {#cohort}

Cohort Analysis

#### Usage

```r
cohort(.data, .date, .value, time_unit = "month", period_label = FALSE)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object |
| `.date` | date column |
| `.value` | id column |
| `time_unit` | do you want summarize the date column to 'day', 'week', 'month','quarter' or 'year' |
| `period_label` | do you want period labels or the dates c(TRUE , FALSE) |

#### Value

segment object

#### Description

Database-friendly cohort analysis function.
A remake of https://github.com/PeerChristensen/cohorts, combining
cohort_table_month, cohort_table_year, and cohort_table_day into a single package.
Rewritten for database compatibility and tested with Snowflake and DuckDB.

#### Details

Groups your .value column by shared time attributes from the .date column.
 Assigns each member to a cohort based on their first entry in .date.
 Aggregates the cohort by the time_unit argument (day, week, month, quarter, or year).
 Computes the distinct count of each cohort member over time.

***

### `cohort_fn()` {#cohort_fn}

Internal function for the cohort segment object

#### Usage

```r
cohort_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | segment object |

#### Value

function

***

### `complete_standard_calendar()` {#complete_standard_calendar}

Title

#### Usage

```r
complete_standard_calendar(.data, x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | DBI object |
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

DBI object

***

### `create_calendar()` {#create_calendar}

Create Calendar Table

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

create_calendar() summarizes a tibble to target time unit and completes the calendar to ensure
no missing days, month, quarter or years. If a grouped tibble is passed through it will complete the calendar
for each combination of the group

#### Details

This is in internal function to make it easier to ensure data has no missing dates to
simplify the use of time intelligence functions downstream of the application.
If you want to summarize to a particular group, simply pass the tibble through to the dplyr::group_by() argument
prior to function and the function will make summarize and make a complete calendar for each group item.

***

### `create_full_dbi()` {#create_full_dbi}

Title

#### Usage

```r
create_full_dbi(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

DBI object

***

### `detect_sql_dialect()` {#detect_sql_dialect}

Detect SQL dialect from a DBI connection

#### Usage

```r
detect_sql_dialect(.con)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.con` | A DBI connection object |

#### Value

A character string: "snowflake", "duckdb", or "postgres"

***

### `dod()` {#dod}

Current period day over previous period day

#### Usage

```r
dod(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the daily value compared to the previous day's value using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()

Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
dod(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type='standard',lag_n=1)
```

***

### `dod_fn()` {#dod_fn}

Day-over-day execution function

#### Usage

```r
dod_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

dod_fn() is the function that is called by dod() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `generate_cli_action()` {#generate_cli_action}

Generate CLI actions

#### Usage

```r
generate_cli_action(x, word)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | input to test against |
| `word` | the key word to validate |

#### Value

list

***

### `generate_retail_calendar()` {#generate_retail_calendar}

Generate a retail (4-4-5, 4-5-4, or 5-4-4) calendar mapping table

#### Usage

```r
generate_retail_calendar(
  start_date,
  end_date,
  calendar_type,
  fiscal_year_start = 1,
  week_start = 7
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `start_date` | Start date (Date or character YYYY-MM-DD) |
| `end_date` | End date (Date or character YYYY-MM-DD) |
| `calendar_type` | One of "445", "454", "544" |
| `fiscal_year_start` | Integer 1-12, month the fiscal year starts nearest to |
| `week_start` | Integer 1-7, day of week (1=Monday, 7=Sunday) |

#### Value

A tibble with columns: date, year, quarter, month, week

***

### `is_yyyy_mm_dd()` {#is_yyyy_mm_dd}

Validate an input is YYYY-MM-DD format

#### Usage

```r
is_yyyy_mm_dd(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | date column |

#### Value

logical

***

### `make_action_cli()` {#make_action_cli}

Make Action field CLI args

#### Usage

```r
make_action_cli(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | action class |

#### Value

list

***

### `make_db_tbl()` {#make_db_tbl}

Make an in memory database from a table

#### Usage

```r
make_db_tbl(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | A tibble, data.frame, or tbl_dbi object. |

#### Value

dbi object

A tbl_dbi object backed by DuckDB.

#### Description

Ensures the input is a database-backed object. If a data.frame is provided,
it is registered into a temporary, in-memory DuckDB instance. If already
a tbl_dbi, it is returned unchanged.

#### Details

When converting a data.frame, this function preserves existing dplyr
groups. It uses DuckDB's duckdb_register, which is a virtual registration
and does not perform a physical copy of the data, making it extremely fast.

***

### `mom()` {#mom}

Current full period month over previous full period month

#### Usage

```r
mom(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the monthly cumulative sum of targeted value using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()

Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
mom(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type='standard',lag_n=1)
```

***

### `mom_fn()` {#mom_fn}

month-over-month execution function

#### Usage

```r
mom_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

mom_fn() is the function that is called by mom() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `momtd()` {#momtd}

Current period month to date compared to previous period month-to-date

#### Usage

```r
momtd(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the monthly cumulative sum of targeted value using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()

Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
momtd(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard", lag_n=1)
```

***

### `momtd_fn()` {#momtd_fn}

Current year to date over previous year-to-date for tibble objects

#### Usage

```r
momtd_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

momtd_fn() is the function that is called by momtd() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `mtd()` {#mtd}

Current period month-to-date

#### Usage

```r
mtd(.data, .date, .value, calendar_type = "standard", fiscal_year_start = 1)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the monthly cumulative sum of targeted value using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()

Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
mtd(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard")
```

***

### `mtd_fn()` {#mtd_fn}

Month-to-date execution function

#### Usage

```r
mtd_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

mtd_fn() is the function that is called by mtd() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `mtdopm()` {#mtdopm}

Current month-to-date over full previous period month

#### Usage

```r
mtdopm(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the monthly cumulative sum of targeted value using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()

Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
mtdopm(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard",lag_n=1)
```

***

### `mtdopm_fn()` {#mtdopm_fn}

Month-to-date over full previous month execution function

#### Usage

```r
mtdopm_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

mtdopm_fn() is the function that is called by mtdopm() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `pmtd()` {#pmtd}

Previous period month-to-date

#### Usage

```r
pmtd(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the monthly cumulative sum of targeted value using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()

Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
pmtd(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard",lag_n=1)
```

***

### `pmtd_fn()` {#pmtd_fn}

Previous month-to-date execution function

#### Usage

```r
pmtd_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

pmtd_fn() is the function that is called by pmtd() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `pqtd()` {#pqtd}

Prior period quarter-to-date

#### Usage

```r
pqtd(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the quarterly cumulative sum of targeted value using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()

use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
pqtd(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard",lag_n=1)
```

***

### `pqtd_fn()` {#pqtd_fn}

Previous quarter-to-date for tibble objects

#### Usage

```r
pqtd_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

pqtd_fn() is the function that is called by pqtd() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `print()` {#print}

Print ti objects

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | A ti object. |
| `...` | Unused. Present for S3/S7 compatibility; additional arguments are ignored. |

#### Value

ti object

segment object

segment object

#### Description

Print ti objects

Print segment objects

Print segment objects

***

### `print_actions_steps()` {#print_actions_steps}

Print action steps

#### Usage

```r
print_actions_steps(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | an S7 class |

#### Value

cli messages

***

### `print_calendar_info()` {#print_calendar_info}

Print calendar information block

#### Usage

```r
print_calendar_info(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti or segment obj |

#### Value

print

***

### `print_fn_info()` {#print_fn_info}

Prints function header info

#### Usage

```r
print_fn_info(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti or segment obj |

#### Value

print

***

### `print_next_steps()` {#print_next_steps}

Prints functions next steps

#### Usage

```r
print_next_steps()
```

#### Value

print

***

### `pwtd()` {#pwtd}

Previous period week-to-date

#### Usage

```r
pwtd(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the weekly cumulative sum of targeted value for the previous week using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()

Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
pwtd(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard",lag_n=1)
```

***

### `pwtd_fn()` {#pwtd_fn}

Previous week-to-date execution function

#### Usage

```r
pwtd_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

pwtd_fn() is the function that is called by pwtd() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `pytd()` {#pytd}

Previous period year-to-date

#### Usage

```r
pytd(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

For each group, pytd()  will create the running annual sum of a value based on the calendar type for the previous year compared to the current year calendar date
 If no period exists, it will return NA
 The function returns a ti object which prints out the summary of steps and actions that will take to create the calendar table and calculations
 Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
pytd(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard",lag_n=1)
```

***

### `pytd_fn()` {#pytd_fn}

Previous year-to-date execution function

#### Usage

```r
pytd_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

pytd_fn() is the function that is called by pytd() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `qoq()` {#qoq}

Current full period quarter over previous full period quarter

#### Usage

```r
qoq(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the full quarter value compared to the previous quarter value respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()
 Use calculate to return the results

#### Examples

```r
qoq(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type='standard',lag_n=1)
```

***

### `qoq_fn()` {#qoq_fn}

Quarter-over-quarter execution function

#### Usage

```r
qoq_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

qoq_fn() is the function that is called by qoq() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `qoqtd()` {#qoqtd}

Current period quarter-to-date compared to previous period quarter-to-date

#### Usage

```r
qoqtd(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the annual cumulative sum of targeted value using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()
 Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
qoqtd(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard",lag_n=1)
```

***

### `qoqtd_fn()` {#qoqtd_fn}

Current quarter to date over previous quarter-to-date for tibble objects

#### Usage

```r
qoqtd_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

qoqtd_fn() is the function that is called by qoqtd() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `qtd()` {#qtd}

Current period quarter-to-date

#### Usage

```r
qtd(.data, .date, .value, calendar_type = "standard", fiscal_year_start = 1)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the quarterly cumulative sum of targeted value using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()

Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
qtd(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard")
```

***

### `qtd_fn()` {#qtd_fn}

Quarter-to-date execution function

#### Usage

```r
qtd_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

qtd_fn() is the function that is called by qtd() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `qtdopq()` {#qtdopq}

Current period quarter-to-date over previous period quarter

#### Usage

```r
qtdopq(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the quarterly cumulative sum of a targeted value and compares it with the previous full quarter's total value respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()
 Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
qtdopq(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type='standard',lag_n=1)
```

***

### `qtdopq_fn()` {#qtdopq_fn}

quarter-to-date over previous quarter execution function

#### Usage

```r
qtdopq_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

qtdopq_fn() is the function that is called by qtdopq() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `seq_date_sql()` {#seq_date_sql}

Generate a Cross-Dialect SQL Date Series

#### Usage

```r
seq_date_sql(
  .con,
  start_date,
  end_date,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  time_unit = "day",
  week_start = 7,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.con` | A valid DBI connection object (e.g., DuckDB, Postgres, Snowflake) or a dbplyr simulated connection. |
| `start_date` | A character string in 'YYYY-MM-DD' format or a Date object representing the start of the series. |
| `end_date` | A character string in 'YYYY-MM-DD' format or a Date object representing the end of the series. |
| `time_unit` | A character string specifying the interval. Must be one of: "day", "week", "month", "quarter", or "year". |
| `week_start` | description |

#### Value

A tbl_lazy (SQL) object with a single column date.

#### Description

Creates a lazy dbplyr table containing a continuous sequence of dates.
The function automatically detects the SQL dialect of the connection and
dispatches the most efficient native series generator (e.g., GENERATE_SERIES
for DuckDB/Postgres or GENERATOR for Snowflake).

#### Details

This function is designed to be "nestable," meaning the resulting SQL can be
used safely inside larger dplyr pipelines. It avoids WITH clauses in
dialects like DuckDB to prevent parser errors when dbplyr wraps the query
in a subquery (e.g., SELECT * FROM (...) AS q01).

For unit testing, the function supports dbplyr simulation objects. If a
TestConnection is detected, it returns a lazy_frame to avoid metadata
field queries that would otherwise fail on a mock connection.

***

### `sql_date_add()` {#sql_date_add}

Generate dialect-appropriate date addition SQL

#### Usage

```r
sql_date_add(.con, unit, n_expr, date_col = "date")
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.con` | A DBI connection object |
| `unit` | A character string: "month", "week", etc. |
| `n_expr` | An expression string for the number of units |
| `date_col` | A character string for the date column name |

#### Value

A dplyr::sql object

***

### `wow()` {#wow}

Current full period week over full previous period week

#### Usage

```r
wow(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the full week value compared to the previous week value using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()

Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
wow(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type='standard',lag_n=1)
```

***

### `wow_fn()` {#wow_fn}

Week-over-week execution function

#### Usage

```r
wow_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

wow_fn() is the function that is called by wow() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `wowtd()` {#wowtd}

Current period Week-to-date over previous period week-to-date

#### Usage

```r
wowtd(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the weekly cumulative sum of targeted value and compares it with the previous week's cumulative sum using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()

Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
wowtd(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard",lag_n=1)
```

***

### `wowtd_fn()` {#wowtd_fn}

Week-to-date over previous week-to-date execution function

#### Usage

```r
wowtd_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

wowtd_fn() is the function that is called by wowtd() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `wtd()` {#wtd}

Current period week-to-date

#### Usage

```r
wtd(.data, .date, .value, calendar_type = "standard", fiscal_year_start = 1)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the weekly cumulative sum of targeted value using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()

Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
wtd(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard")
```

***

### `wtd_fn()` {#wtd_fn}

Week-to-date execution function

#### Usage

```r
wtd_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

wtd_fn() is the function that is called by wtd() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `wtdopw()` {#wtdopw}

Current period week-to-date over full previous period week

#### Usage

```r
wtdopw(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the weekly cumulative sum of targeted value and compares it with the full previous week's total using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()

Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
wtdopw(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard",lag_n=1)
```

***

### `wtdopw_fn()` {#wtdopw_fn}

Week-to-date over full previous week execution function

#### Usage

```r
wtdopw_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

wtdopw_fn() is the function that is called by wtdopw() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `yoy()` {#yoy}

Current full period year over previous full period year

#### Usage

```r
yoy(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the full year value compared to the previous year value respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()
 Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
yoy(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type='standard',lag_n=1)
```

***

### `yoy_fn()` {#yoy_fn}

Current year-to-date over previous period year-to-date execution function

#### Usage

```r
yoy_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

yoy_fn() is the function that is called by yoy() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `yoytd()` {#yoytd}

Current period year-to-date compared to previous period year-to-date

#### Usage

```r
yoytd(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the annual cumulative sum of targeted value and compares it with the previous period's annual cumulative to date sum using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()
 Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
yoytd(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard",lag_n=1)
```

***

### `yoytd_fn()` {#yoytd_fn}

Current year to date over previous year-to-date execution function

#### Usage

```r
yoytd_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

yoytd_fn() is the function that is called by yoytd() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `ytd()` {#ytd}

Current period year-to-date

#### Usage

```r
ytd(.data, .date, .value, calendar_type = "standard", fiscal_year_start = 1)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

For each group, ytd()  will create the running annual sum of a value based on the calendar type specified
 The function returns a ti object which prints out the summary of steps and actions that will take to create the calendar table and calculations
 Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
ytd(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type="standard")
```

***

### `ytd_fn()` {#ytd_fn}

Year-to-date execution function

#### Usage

```r
ytd_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

ytd_fn() is the function that is called by ytd() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object with dplyr::collect()

***

### `ytdopy()` {#ytdopy}

Current period year-to-date compared to full previous period

#### Usage

```r
ytdopy(
  .data,
  .date,
  .value,
  calendar_type = "standard",
  lag_n = 1,
  fiscal_year_start = 1
)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `.data` | tibble or dbi object (either grouped or ungrouped) |
| `.date` | the date column to group by |
| `.value` | the value column to summarize |
| `calendar_type` | select either 'standard', '445', '454', or '544' calendar, see 'Details' for additional information |
| `lag_n` | the number of periods to lag |
| `fiscal_year_start` | integer 1-12, the month the fiscal year starts nearest to (default 1 = January). Only used with retail calendars ('445', '454', '544'). |

#### Value

ti object

#### Description

This calculates the full year value compared to the previous year value using a standard or 5-5-4 calendar respecting
any groups that are passed through with dplyr::group_by()
 Use calculate to return the results

#### Details

This function creates a complete calendar object that fills in any missing days, weeks, months, quarters, or years
 If you provide a grouped object with dplyr::group_by(), it will generate a complete calendar for each group
 The function creates a ti object, which pre-processes the data and arguments for further downstream functions

standard calendar

 The standard calendar splits the year into 12 months (with 28–31 days each) and uses a 7-day week
 It automatically accounts for leap years every four years to match the Gregorian calendar

5-5-4 calendar

 The 5-5-4 calendar divides the fiscal year into 52 weeks (occasionally 53), organizing each quarter into two 5-week periods and one 4-week period.
 This system is commonly used in retail and financial reporting

#### Examples

```r
ytdopy(contoso::sales,.date=order_date,.value=quantity,calendar_type='standard',lag_n=1)
```

***

### `ytdopy_fn()` {#ytdopy_fn}

Year-to-date over full prior period year

#### Usage

```r
ytdopy_fn(x)
```

#### Arguments

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `x` | ti object |

#### Value

dbi object

#### Description

ytdopy_fn() is the function that is called by ytdopy() when passed through to calculate

#### Details

This is internal non exported function that is nested in ti class and is called upon when the underlying function is called
by calculate
This will return a dbi object that can converted to a tibble object withdplyr::collect()

***
